policy_statement: Remodel aquaculture infrastructures to optimise water consumption and reduce losses.
mechanism_description: Remodelling aquaculture infrastructure improves water-use efficiency and reduces system losses, lowering overall water consumption and improving water resource resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Same aquaculture output → ↓ water input/withdrawals (via ↓ losses + ↑ efficiency) → ↓ resource demand and improved system resilience to water scarcity
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Aquaculture operators upgrade/remodel ponds, tanks, raceways, pipes, liners, valves, recirculation systems, and monitoring/controls to reduce leakage, evaporation, and inefficient flow-through use.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Improved containment and recirculation reduce physical water losses (seepage/leaks/overflow) and reduce make-up water requirements; better controls optimize water exchange rates while maintaining water quality.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower total freshwater abstraction/consumption per unit production, reducing stress on local water resources under drought and warming; potential secondary benefits include reduced energy use for pumping/treatment (and associated GHG reductions) where applicable.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced freshwater withdrawals and losses from aquaculture systems (improved water-use efficiency), easing resource stress under climate variability.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Infrastructure upgrades → reduced water losses/withdrawals → improved efficiency of resource use (water).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While improved water management can aid adaptation (resilience to drought) and may reduce energy/GHG indirectly, the direct and largest effect across policies using this mechanism is reduced resource input (water) for the same service/output, which is Resource Efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: If upgrades explicitly target energy reduction (e.g., pump electrification/efficiency) or are framed mainly as drought adaptation infrastructure, the policy could have strong mitigation/adaptation co-benefits; however, the canonical mechanism as defined is water-use and loss reduction, keeping Resource Efficiency dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that remodels aquaculture infrastructure to optimize consumption and reduce losses inherently prioritizes reducing water input per output, matching Resource Efficiency as the primary category.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: More granular subtype assignment (e.g., mitigation co-benefit via reduced pumping energy; adaptation benefit via drought robustness) is deferred to policy-level evidence and wording.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could shift toward Mitigation if the upgrade is primarily about reducing energy for pumping/aeration with quantified GHG cuts, or toward Adaptation if framed explicitly as drought/heat-risk management infrastructure; absent such emphasis, classify as Resource Efficiency.
key_indicators: “Remodelling of aquaculture infrastructures”; “optimise water consumption”; “reduce losses”
co_benefits: Potential reduced energy use for pumping/treatment (and associated GHG reductions); improved operational efficiency/cost savings; potentially improved water quality management
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit instrument is named (e.g., subsidy, mandate, standard); classification assumes the policy action is implemented via funding/implementation of physical retrofits (infrastructure investment) rather than purely guidance or regulation.
